utterances: (Soundbite of music)
(As Forrest Gump) Momma always says there's an awful lot you can tell about a person by their shoes - where they're going, where they've been.
It's hard to think, though, that an FHA program which is experiencing such huge growth and is picking up the slack from a decimated subprime market is not going to see increased losses down the road.
We just don't know what the price tag is going to be.
Some people are even calling FHA loans the new subprime because they have the most lax lending guidelines available now.
It's terrible.
I don't know why people would say that.
It's so untrue.
Meg Burns is the director of single family program development with FHA.
We validate that they have the employment.
We require that the income be sufficient to cover the cost of the mortgage.
These are two key features that were missing in the subprime market.
So we are certainly nowhere close to what was happening in subprime.
That said, FHA is anticipating more of the loans it insures will fall into foreclosure in the coming months.
That's why it's shifting $12 billion into a loss reserve fund.
Tamara Keith, NPR News, Washington.
This is MORNING EDITION from NPR News.
I'm Lynn Neary in for Renee Montagne.
And I'm Steve Inskeep.
Pope Benedict XVI is not the only high-profile visitor in Washington this morning.
The other visitor is not expected to fill a baseball stadium for a morning Mass, but he will be meeting all three presidential candidates.
British Prime Minister Gordon Brown has already met with leading bankers in New York.
That meeting that came after he spoke with top bank executives in London.
NPR's Rob Gifford is in London tracking Brown's effort to restore economic confidence.
And, Rob is Britain is having the same housing and mortgage problems that the United States is?
It is having some similar problems.
It hasn't had quite the same subprime meltdown, Steve, that you've seen across in the United States.
But Gordon Brown has come under intense pressure because there is the same kind of credit crunch going on.
The boom is over - the boom, which, incidentally, Gordon Brown presided over as chancellor of the exchequer, head of the Treasury here for a decade.
He stepped into power when Tony Blair leaves and, bam, he's hit by the credit crunch and he's getting a lot of the blame for that.
Okay.
So, is this guy, who is an economic expert, doing anything that's helping?
Well, that's the problem.
I mean, to be fair to Gordon Brown it's not all his fault, of course.
This is a global phenomenon.
But when it starts to squeeze people, who do they look to blame?
They look to blame the government.
What we're seeing now is that the treasury here is trying to get some moves in process to try and ease the crunch.
But some people are just saying it's taking so long and the government really needs to move quicker.
I suppose we should say that not only does Britain have a very large economy - not as large as the U.S.
but very large - but Britain looms very large in the world of finance.
So, can these two leaders - Gordon Brown and President Bush, who he's meeting today also - do anything together to fight this crisis?
That's the big question.
Perhaps the upcoming meeting with Ben Bernanke may even be more important, the head of the Fed.
Gordon Brown also met with senior Wall Street bank executives yesterday.
The meeting with George Bush will be important symbolically.
I think to some extent some of these issues are outside the control of world leaders.

But certainly from Gordon Brown's point of view, especially in the twilight of the Bush years, there's an element of Brown going to Washington in order to be seen to be doing something when in fact it's going to take some time for this to all play out and he can't just snap his magic wand, of course, and make this credit crunch go away.
When Gordon Brown sits across the table from George W.
Bush, do you think that Bush is looking at an ally who is as strong on issues like Iraq as Tony Blair was?
Absolutely not.
I think the Blair/Bush coordination, the partnership there, was very strong.
Brown is a very different man.
He's often described as the duel Scotsman, he's not as charismatic, and certainly he's not as interventionist as Tony Blair was and indeed George Bush is.
And so I don't think there is nearly such a meeting of minds between the two men.

And in fact, crucially also today Gordon Brown is meeting today with all three possible candidates - Senator McCain, Barack Obama and Hillary Clinton in order to look to the future, because one of those is likely to be the one he'll actually be dealing with on a day-to-day basis come early next year.
Is it too rude to point out that when President Bush and Prime Minister Brown get into a room you've got two men with appalling approval ratings?
No, I think that's absolutely true.
I think the problem for Gordon Brown, though, is that President Bush has served his two terms; he knows he's on the way out.
Gordon Brown has not yet got a mandate from the British people.
So he has to go to the British people in a general election.
He took over from Tony Blair simply when Tony Blair stepped down.
Within the next two years Gordon Brown must go to the British people, and that is a very, very big concern for him with the economy as bad as he is and his poll ratings as bad as they are.
Rob, always good to hear from you.
Thank you very much, Steve.
NPR's London correspondent Rob Gifford.
We're joined now by someone with much experience dealing with North Korea.
Evans Revere was the state department's main liaison with North Korea from 1998 to 2000, and then charge d'affair at the U.S.
Embassy in Seoul.
Mr. Revere, welcome to the program.
It's a delight to be here.
And I wanted to ask you first, I gather that you were involved - prior to President Clinton making his trip - involved with helping one of the journalist's families negotiate with North Korea.
What can you tell us about that?
And where it stops curving and goes straight, it's just been renamed Major Taylor Boulevard.
Oh, there it is.
Major Taylor Boulevard.
And it's been a lot of hard work and a lot of blood, sweat and tears that my kids have put into these projects.
They're not just livestock.
Goats are very personable, very lovable.
So these goats were a part of our family.
You know, we cared for them day-in, day-out.
It's just heartbreaking and devastating for a 12- and 14-year-old.
Police say owners have provided good descriptions of the missing animals, and they think there may be one person or crew behind the thefts.
In the absence of solid leads, the sheriff's department's Tony Botti advises the public in Fresno to stay vigilant and do their best to protect their herds.
From NPR News, this is NEWS & NOTES.
I'm Farai Chideya.
Senate Democrats forced a marathon session on Capitol Hill Tuesday evening.
Republicans have refused to allow a vote on the withdrawal of U.S.
troops from Iraq, so Democrats hoped to force the issue with a late-night debate.
Their critics say the whole event was just bad theater.
For more, we've got Mary Frances Berry, a professor of history at the University of Pennsylvania.
And Ron Christie, vice president of the lobbying firm D.C.
Navigators, also a former special assistant to President George W.
Bush.
Mary, I'm going to jump right in with you.
So what exactly happened last night?

Well, the Democrats said, which what I thought was some pretty good theater, which replicated what happened in 2003 when the Republicans had in all-nighter to try to end the filibuster on judicial appointments, which they didn't end either.
Same trappings, same everything, same complaints about is it theater.
And I think they achieved their objective.
Of course, they didn't get a positive vote to end the filibuster.
But what they were trying to do was to show the anti-war voters why the Democrats have not been able to stop the war because many of them were confused.
The Democrats are down in the polls.
Democrats in Congress are down in the polls.
And they've been blamed and attacked for not ending this war by people who voted for them in 2006.
So I think they achieved the educational objective of trying to show people why, even though the House voted to do what the Senate was not able to get a vote.
So you say that, in a way, this is about the Democrats trying to show their voters, look, we were trying our best here.
Is there any chance that the Republicans are going to take a hit for this?
Or do you think it's not going to be - it's going to be, if anything, maybe a show of strength for Republican voters from their representatives?
Well, some Republicans did join the Democrats on the final vote, although, not enough.
And many Republicans who have spoken out in recent days against the war still didn't vote here.
They've been at home making speeches about how they're against everything.
But I think on the last time it happened in 2003, it was part of the impetus to help defeat Tom Daschle when he was majority leader.
When he was leader of the Democrats, it helped to defeat him in South Dakota.
I know that there is a push to try to attack Mitch McConnell in Kentucky.
And maybe some of the people who saw it and see that no vote can be taken may oppose to the some of the Republican candidates and it might move the discussion along.
The part of this was trying to move the ball forward incrementally some place to get a change in policy.
Well, you know, Mary, I just want to ask you a little bit about the theater aspect of this.
What happens to the good old days when Jimmy Stewart had to stand up all by himself?
I understand the senators brought in cots, snacks.
Who knows, maybe, they even had foot massages?
Well, I think that it would have been great if people had stood up there, like Jimmy Stewart, and talked for hours and hours and read things from the Bible.
And I've seen some filibusters in the early years like that.
But this is a more genteel, civilized kind of theater, which is not as hard on the senators.
And it's not as hard either, I guess, on the viewers who were committed - who sit there at the watch and sleeping on the cots, I guess - if anybody slept on them - helped.
So it was theater.
But it was not as gripping as Jimmy Stewart.
Well, I want to bring in Ron.
So, we've been talking about both the theatrical aspects of this and also the political aspects of this.
Do you think that the Republicans made a point?
Or do you think the Republicans took a hit in this one?
Well, I think they made a point.

The point being here, Farai, is that for whatever reason, there are number of people in the United States Senate on the other side of the political aisle who were trying to exert their will to bring the troops home before General Petraeus is coming out with his report in September and before the troops have had the opportunity to complete their mission.
